name: Sum Array
description: Given an array of numbers, return the sum of all elements. The function should handle the empty array (return 0). The signature is `def sum_array(arr): ...` (or equivalent).
rubric_structured:
1. Correctness (60%): The function returns the sum of all elements correctly for both populated and empty arrays. No IndexError, RecursionError, or wrong-answer logic. Judge the algorithm the code demonstrates, not the literal character sequence — if a one-token typo (e.g. missing `:`, missing indent) is the only problem, Correctness still reflects the demonstrated intent.
2. Code Quality (25%): Pythonic style. Function and variable names are descriptive and do not shadow built-ins like `sum`. The chosen approach (loop, built-in, `functools.reduce`, recursion) is implemented cleanly without unnecessary intermediate variables. The code parses without SyntaxError; a localized parse error in otherwise readable code is a bounded deduction here (see the system −5/100 cap), not a Correctness zero.
3. Efficiency (15%): The chosen approach is reasonable for the input size — a single-pass O(n) accumulation. No needless duplicate work, multiplication, or per-iteration overhead.
rubric_unstructured: Score the submission on correctness (60%), code quality (25%), and efficiency (15%). Correctness is judged on the algorithm the code demonstrates — the function should return the sum of all elements for both populated and empty arrays without IndexError, RecursionError, or wrong-answer logic; a localized syntax error in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness. The code should be Pythonic — descriptive names, no shadowing of built-ins like `sum`, and clean implementation of the chosen approach (loop, built-in, reduce, recursion); parse-error hygiene lives here and is bounded by the system −5/100 cap when the error is localized. The approach should be a reasonable single-pass O(n) accumulation without unnecessary work or duplication.

name: Palindrome Check
description: Implement `boolean isPalindrome(String s)` that returns `true` if the input reads the same forwards and backwards, ignoring case and non-alphanumeric characters, and `false` otherwise. The empty string is a palindrome. Examples: `"A man, a plan, a canal: Panama"` → `true`; `"race a car"` → `false`; `""` → `true`.
rubric_structured:
1. Correctness (60%): Returns `true` exactly when the input reads the same forward and backward after lower-casing and stripping non-alphanumeric characters. Handles the empty string, mixed-case inputs, and inputs containing punctuation. No off-by-one errors on odd-length strings, no silent always-true logic. Judge the algorithm the code demonstrates, not the literal character sequence — a localized compile error (missing semicolon, swapped return type, stray `}`) in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness.
2. Code Quality (25%): Idiomatic Java. Uses `.equals` rather than `==` for string comparison. The chosen approach (two pointers, reverse-and-compare, StringBuilder, Stream API, or recursion) is implemented cleanly with descriptive names. The code compiles without errors; a localized compile error in otherwise readable code is a bounded deduction here (see the system −5/100 cap), not a Correctness zero.
3. Efficiency (15%): Avoids quadratic substring recursion and stack-blowing implementations on long inputs. Does not allocate more strings than the chosen approach requires.
rubric_unstructured: Score the `isPalindrome` method on correctness (60%), code quality (25%), and efficiency (15%). Correctness is judged on the algorithm the code demonstrates — it should correctly return `true` exactly when the input reads the same forward and backward after lower-casing and stripping non-alphanumeric characters, including the empty string and inputs with mixed case or punctuation, with no off-by-one errors on odd-length strings and no silent always-true logic; a localized compile error in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness. The Java should be idiomatic — uses `.equals` rather than `==`, applies a clean approach (two pointers, reverse-and-compare, StringBuilder, Streams, or recursion), and reads cleanly; compile hygiene lives here and is bounded by the system −5/100 cap when the error is localized. The implementation should not be quadratic or stack-overflow-prone, and should not allocate more strings than the chosen approach requires.

name: LRU Cache
description: Design an in-memory Least Recently Used (LRU) cache as a class `LRUCache<K, V>` with constructor `(capacity: number)`, `get(key: K): V | undefined`, `put(key: K, value: V): void` (overwriting if present), and `size(): number`. Both `get` and `put` count as 'use'. When `size > capacity` after a `put`, evict the least-recently-used entry. Both operations should be O(1) amortized.
rubric_structured:
1. Correctness (40%): `get` returns the value or `undefined` for missing keys; `put` overwrites and refreshes recency; both `get` and `put` count as use; eviction removes the LEAST-recently-used entry when `size > capacity`. Returns `undefined` (not `null`) for misses. Handles `capacity = 0` without infinite loops. Does not silently mis-evict the most-recently-used entry. Judge the algorithm the code demonstrates, not the literal character sequence — a localized TypeScript compile error in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness.
2. Data Structures (25%): The structure used can deliver the recency semantics — `Map` (which preserves insertion order in TypeScript), or hash + doubly-linked list, or equivalent. No duplicate recency entries that corrupt eviction order. No abuse of plain object as ordered map.
3. Efficiency (20%): `get` and `put` are O(1) amortized in the canonical solution. No O(n) scans on every access (e.g., array `indexOf` to find the LRU). No accidental re-allocation on every `put`.
4. Code Quality (15%): Uses generics `<K, V>` rather than `any`. Picks a clean approach and implements it idiomatically without redundant fields or awkward APIs. The code compiles; a localized compile error in otherwise readable code is a bounded deduction here (see the system −5/100 cap), not a Correctness zero.
rubric_unstructured: Score the LRUCache on correctness (40%), data-structure choice (25%), efficiency (20%), and code quality (15%). Correctness is judged on the algorithm the code demonstrates — it should implement the spec faithfully (`get` returns the value or `undefined`, `put` overwrites and refreshes recency, both `get` and `put` count as use, the LEAST-recently-used entry is evicted once `size > capacity`, `undefined` (not `null`) is returned for misses, `capacity = 0` does not blow up, and the most-recently-used entry is never mis-evicted); a localized compile error in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness. The data structure should be one that can deliver the recency semantics — `Map` (insertion-ordered in TypeScript), or hash + doubly-linked list, or equivalent — without duplicate recency entries and without abusing plain object as an ordered map. Both `get` and `put` should be O(1) amortized; no O(n) scans like `indexOf` to find the LRU, and no accidental re-allocation on every `put`. Stylistically, use generics `<K, V>` rather than `any`, and implement the chosen approach idiomatically without redundant fields or awkward APIs; compile hygiene lives here and is bounded by the system −5/100 cap when the error is localized.

name: Dijkstra Shortest Path
description: Implement Dijkstra's single-source shortest-path algorithm with signature `std::vector<long long> dijkstra(int n, const std::vector<std::vector<std::pair<int, int>>>& graph, int source)`. `n` is the number of nodes (0..n-1); `graph[u]` is a list of `{v, w}` outgoing edges with non-negative weights. Return shortest distances from `source` to every node, with `LLONG_MAX` for unreachable nodes. Edge weights fit in `int`; distances accumulate in `long long`.
rubric_structured:
1. Correctness (45%): The algorithm is actually Dijkstra (not BFS, DFS, or Bellman-Ford). Returns shortest distances from `source` to every node, with `LLONG_MAX` (or equivalent sentinel) for unreachable nodes. Initializes `dist[source] = 0`. Does not crash on `source` out of range. Handles negative weights either by failing fast or by documenting the precondition. Judge the algorithm the code demonstrates, not the literal character sequence — a localized compile error (missing `#include`, missing closing brace, wrong template-parameter count) in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness.
2. Efficiency (25%): Skips stale heap entries to keep the run time at O((V+E) log V). Uses `long long` for accumulated distances (avoids overflow). Does not re-process settled nodes. Does not iterate the full graph for every relaxation.
3. Data Structures (15%): Uses `std::priority_queue` (min-heap) or `std::set` for the frontier — not a linear scan or unsorted list. Adjacency list is consumed correctly (no transpose mistakes).
4. Code Quality (15%): Readable C++ with meaningful names for the distance array, frontier, and edge endpoints. Logic flows clearly without redundant work or dead branches. Any sentinel choice (`LLONG_MAX`, `std::numeric_limits<long long>::max()`, or a documented constant) is acceptable as long as it is used consistently and does not overflow when compared against `dist[u] + w`. Stylistic choices like `using namespace std` or `.first`/`.second` vs structured bindings are not penalized. The code compiles without errors; a localized compile error in otherwise readable code is a bounded deduction here (see the system −5/100 cap), not a Correctness zero.
rubric_unstructured: Score the Dijkstra implementation on correctness (45%), efficiency (25%), data-structure choice (15%), and code quality (15%). Correctness is judged on the algorithm the code demonstrates — the code should actually run Dijkstra (not BFS, DFS, or Bellman-Ford), return shortest distances for every node with `LLONG_MAX` for unreachable ones, initialize `dist[source] = 0`, not crash on out-of-range source, and fail clearly or document negative-weight handling; a localized compile error in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness. Performance-wise it should skip stale heap entries (O((V+E) log V)), use `long long` for accumulation to avoid overflow, not re-process settled nodes, and not iterate the whole graph per relaxation. The frontier should be a `std::priority_queue` or `std::set` rather than a linear scan, and the adjacency list should be consumed correctly without transpose mistakes. Code quality is about readability: meaningful names for the distance array, frontier, and edge endpoints, clear control flow, and a consistent sentinel choice that doesn't overflow when added to a distance. `LLONG_MAX` is fine, `std::numeric_limits<long long>::max()` is fine, and stylistic preferences like `using namespace std` or `.first`/`.second` vs structured bindings are not penalized; compile hygiene also lives here and is bounded by the system −5/100 cap when the error is localized.

name: Binary Search Tree
description: Implement a Binary Search Tree for `Integer` keys with the following API: `void insert(int key)` (idempotent on duplicates), `boolean contains(int key)`, `boolean delete(int key)` returning `true` iff the key existed, `List<Integer> inorder()` returning keys in ascending order, and `int size()`. `delete` must correctly handle the leaf, single-child, and two-children (replace with in-order successor or predecessor and remove that successor) cases.
rubric_structured:
1. Correctness (40%): All five methods behave per the spec. `insert` is idempotent on duplicates. `delete` correctly handles leaf, single-child, and two-children cases (replace with in-order successor or predecessor and remove that successor afterwards). `inorder` returns keys in ascending order. `contains` and `size` are correct. No NullPointerException on contains/delete of missing keys. Judge the algorithm the code demonstrates, not the literal character sequence — a localized compile error in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness.
2. Data Structures (30%): A genuine BST with `Node { key, left, right }` honouring the BST invariant. `Node` is a private inner class with private fields (encapsulated), not public. Does not delegate to `java.util.TreeSet` / `TreeMap` as a hidden cheat that defeats the spirit of the assignment. The chosen approach (recursive, iterative) is applied consistently across methods.
3. Efficiency (15%): `size()` is O(1) (cached), not recomputed by walking the tree on every call. `inorder` does not concatenate strings or repeatedly resize a fixed-size array. Avoids stack overflow on long degenerate inputs, or the limitation is acknowledged.
4. Code Quality (15%): Idiomatic Java. Uses `List<Integer>` (interface) rather than `ArrayList<Integer>` in return types. Methods have clear names and consistent recursion / iteration style. The code compiles; a localized compile error in otherwise readable code is a bounded deduction here (see the system −5/100 cap), not a Correctness zero.
rubric_unstructured: Score the BST on correctness (40%), data-structure design (30%), efficiency (15%), and code quality (15%). Correctness is judged on the algorithm the code demonstrates — all five methods should behave per spec (`insert` is idempotent on duplicates; `delete` correctly handles the leaf, single-child, and two-children cases including removing the successor afterwards; `inorder` returns keys ascending; `contains` and `size` return correct values; no NullPointerException on missing keys); a localized compile error in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness. The data structure should be a genuine BST with an encapsulated private `Node` inner class (private fields), the BST invariant should be honoured, and the implementation must not delegate to `java.util.TreeSet` / `TreeMap`. Performance-wise, `size()` should be O(1) cached rather than recomputed by tree-walk, `inorder` should not rely on string concatenation or repeatedly resize fixed-size arrays, and degenerate inputs should not blow the stack (or the limitation should be acknowledged). Stylistically, the Java should be idiomatic — `List<Integer>` in return types, clear method names, and consistent recursion or iteration style; compile hygiene lives here and is bounded by the system −5/100 cap when the error is localized.

name: Concurrent Worker Pool
description: Implement `func ParallelMap[T, R any](inputs []T, workers int, fn func(T) R) []R` that processes the inputs concurrently using `workers` goroutines while preserving input order — `out[i]` must be the result of applying `fn` to `inputs[i]`. Treat `workers <= 0` as `workers = 1`. When `workers > len(inputs)`, only `len(inputs)` goroutines should be spawned. The function must not deadlock, leak goroutines, or panic on empty input. `fn` is safe to call concurrently.
rubric_structured:
1. Correctness (35%): Output preserves input order (`out[i]` corresponds to `inputs[i]`). Treats `workers <= 0` as `workers = 1`. Spawns at most `len(inputs)` goroutines. Does not panic on empty input. Judge the algorithm the code demonstrates, not the literal character sequence — a localized compile error (missing `import`, missing `any` constraint on a generic) in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness.
2. Concurrency Safety (35%): No data races (`go test -race` clean). No goroutine leaks when `workers > len(inputs)` or on empty input. No deadlock on capacity-zero channels. Does not capture the loop variable by reference in goroutines. Synchronization is correct — every goroutine that is spawned is also waited for.
3. Efficiency (15%): Actually parallelises — does not serialise behind a single mutex on the result slice when indexed writes are already race-free. Channel and slice capacities sized to avoid unnecessary blocking. Number of goroutines is bounded by `min(workers, len(inputs))`.
4. Code Quality (15%): Idiomatic Go concurrency — channels and goroutines used cleanly, `sync.WaitGroup` for synchronization, no unnecessary external dependencies (e.g., pulling in `errgroup` when no errors are involved), no superfluous mutexes when indexed slice writes are race-free. The code compiles; a localized compile error in otherwise readable code is a bounded deduction here (see the system −5/100 cap), not a Correctness zero.
rubric_unstructured: Score `ParallelMap` on correctness (35%), concurrency safety (35%), efficiency (15%), and code quality (15%). Correctness is judged on the algorithm the code demonstrates — the output slice must preserve input order (`out[i]` corresponds to `inputs[i]`); `workers <= 0` must be treated as `workers = 1`; at most `len(inputs)` goroutines should be spawned; and the code must not panic on empty input. A localized compile error in otherwise readable code does not zero out Correctness. Concurrency safety is critical — no data races (`go test -race` clean), no goroutine leaks when `workers > len(inputs)` or on empty input, no deadlock on capacity-zero channels, no loop-variable-capture bug, and every spawned goroutine must be waited for. The implementation should actually parallelise — no serialisation behind a mutex when indexed writes are already race-free, and channel / slice capacities should be sized to avoid unnecessary blocking, with goroutine count bounded by `min(workers, len(inputs))`. The Go style should be idiomatic — channels and goroutines used cleanly, `sync.WaitGroup` for synchronization, no superfluous mutexes, and no unnecessary external dependencies; compile hygiene lives here and is bounded by the system −5/100 cap when the error is localized.
